name: What to Do After a Warehouse Accident: Your Step-by-Step Guide to Protecting Your Rights
description: What to Do After a Warehouse Accident: Your Step-by-Step Guide to Protecting Your Rights - Salt Lake City

step.name: Step 1
step.text: Warehouse accidents are chaotic, painful, and confusing. Whether you were struck by a forklift, fell from a ladder, or felt your back "pop" while lifting a heavy pallet, the actions you take in the first few hours will determine whether the insurance company pays your bills or leaves you in debt.

step.name: Step 2
step.text: ### 1. Report the Injury Immediately (And in Writing)

step.name: Step 3
step.text: The most common mistake warehouse workers make is thinking, "I'll just sleep on it and see if it feels better." In Utah and Idaho, you have strict deadlines for reporting. Even if your supervisor "knows" you got hurt, go to the office and ask to fill out a formal incident report. If they won't give you one, send a text or email to your boss saying: "I am reporting that I hurt my [body part] today at [time] while [what you were doing]." This creates a digital trail that cannot be deleted.

step.name: Step 5
step.text: Do not wait. Go to the nearest instacare or emergency room. Tell the intake desk clearly: "This is a work-related injury." This ensures the billing is routed correctly. Most importantly, describe the accident exactly as it happened. If you mention a "sore back" but forget to mention the forklift hit you, the insurance company will use that inconsistency to deny the claim later.

step.name: Step 7
step.text: Warehouses change fast. Spills are mopped up, broken pallets are thrown away, and forklift brakes are magically "fixed" the next day. If you are physically able, take pictures of the scene. Who else was in the aisle? Write down their names. If a piece of equipment failed, try to get a photo of the serial number or unit ID.

step.name: Step 9
step.text: If the doctor tells you to stay off your feet, stay off your feet. If they give you "light duty" restrictions, do not exceed them—even if your supervisor tells you to "just help out this one time." If the insurance company catches you lifting heavy items or working against medical advice, they will cut off your checks immediately.

step.name: Step 11
step.text: Workers\' compensation is a specialized field. A general "injury lawyer" may not understand the specific rules of the Utah Labor Commission or the Idaho Industrial Commission. At Just Work Comp Law, we offer free consultations to help you understand if your claim is being handled fairly. Call us at 801-746-0290 before you sign any settlement papers.
